category: Aggregate handicraft exports (non-carpet-specific, all craft types combined)
description: Iran exported $427 million worth of handicrafts during the first eleven months of Iranian calendar year 1398, of which ~$190 million was generated through 'suitcase trade' (informal cross-border personal-luggage trade) across 20 provinces -- the pre-COVID baseline for this series.
Handicraft exports collapsed to $120 million for the same 11-month window in 1399 -- a ~72% year-over-year drop explicitly attributed by the article to COVID-19 pandemic damage to global trade.
Handicraft exports recovered to $400 million for the first 10 months of 1401, representing roughly 30% growth over the same period the prior year -- post-COVID recovery.

Handicraft exports totaled $223 million in formal channels for the Iranian year ending 20 March 2025, with an estimated equivalent amount (~$223M) additionally moved through informal 'suitcase trade' by travelers -- meaning total (formal+informal) handicraft trade may be roughly double the formal figure, a pattern consistent across this whole series.

Deputy Minister of Cultural Heritage/Tourism/Handicrafts Maryam Jalali cited Iran's handicraft exports as running at ~$400 million annually as of Dec 2025. Key export products named: woven handicrafts, engraved metalwork, wooden items, leather embroidery -- to neighboring countries primarily. Government allocated 10x more budget to handicraft support in 2025 vs. 2024 (relative multiplier only, no absolute figure given).
source_name: Tehran Times (state media, labeled) via TV BRICS syndication

description: 570,000 licensed craftsmen registered nationally across '300 fields of art' (elsewhere reported as '299 recognized branches of handicrafts, classified into 18 groups') under Iran's state handicraft-licensing system.

category: UNESCO Creative Cities Network -- Crafts & Folk Art category
description: Isfahan and Bandar Abbas are Iran's UNESCO Creative Cities in the 'Crafts and Folk Art' category (independently verified via unesco.org, not just the Iranian state-media claim of '14 cities + 3 villages registered globally' which could not be corroborated this round). Isfahan's creative sector spans 167 distinct craft disciplines incl. carpet weaving, metalwork, woodwork, ceramics, painting, and inlay work (khatam-kari). Bandar Abbas's crafts are grouped into practical/marine crafts, local clothing, and local musical instruments.

Reza Shah established state-run traditional-craft schools in major Persian cities in the 1930s specifically to preserve and encourage local production of textiles and other handwork -- the earliest institutional anchor found for modern Iranian handicraft policy.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica - 'CRAFTS' (via WebSearch index snippet; live page Cloudflare-blocked, consistent with every other iranicaonline.org page touched in this project)

category: Foreign-trained artists revive traditional craft techniques
description: Foreign-trained Persian art students returning home in the 1960s sought to revive historical styles/techniques of Persian handicrafts, a second institutional wave after the 1930s Reza Shah schools; Tehran markets by this era served tourists, connoisseurs, ordinary household buyers, and foreign importers simultaneously.
source_name: Encyclopaedia Iranica - 'CRAFTS' (via WebSearch index snippet; live page Cloudflare-blocked)
